coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped carbon dots based fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive detection of thiram in hawthorn
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Lizhu
Hou, Yujiao
Guo, Haipeng
Liu, Chang
Li, Ying
Jia, Boyu
Zhou, Lidong
Kong, Weijun
coredata.dc:description: Development of highly efficient and sensitive methods for trace detection of pesticides in foods is in great need. In this study, a signal “on–off-on” fluorescent nanosensor was proposed based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and biomass derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for accurate qualitation and quantitation of thiram in hawthorn. The water-soluble orange peel-derived N-CDs were obtained through a facile one-step microwave-assisted synthesis strategy. The fluorescence of biomass N-CDs could be effectively quenched by AuNPs through inner filter effect (IFE), and recovered when AuNPs were aggregated. In the presence of thiram, N-CDs specially bound with AuNPs through Au-S bonds, causing the aggregation of AuNPs with a visible color change from red to blue, accompanying with the release of N-CDs and a recovery of fluorescence. The restored fluorescence intensity was relevant with the added amount of thiram in a concentration-dependent manner for reliable quantitation. Under the optimized conditions, the newly-developed fluorescent nanosensor exhibited high selectivity for thiram out of other six interfering pesticides, as well as excellent sensitivity with an ultralow detection limit of 4.7 ng/mL and a wide detection range of 10–200 ng/mL. The practical application of the established nanosensor in the spiked hawthorn samples confirmed satisfactory recoveries of 102.22–107.57% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 5%. The results indicated the feasibility, practicality and wide application prospect of the proposed fluorescent nanosensor for thiram in foods and other commodities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanosensor
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
One-step microwave-assisted synthesis
Gold nanoparticles
Thiram

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification of biomass N-CDs
Fluorescent sensing of thiram in standard solution
Optimization of synthesis conditions of biomass N-CDs
Characterization of the synthesized biomass N-CDs
Sensing mechanism of the fluorescent nanosensor for thiram detection
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNPs on N-CDs
Fluorescence recovery mechanism of thiram on N-CDs
Optimization of sensing conditions for thiram
Selectivity of the AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization regarding (A) The TEM images, (B) The HR-TEM images, (C) The distribution histogram of corresponding size, and (D) The XRD pattern of biomass N-CDs.
XPS spectra of (A) full scan, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s of biomass N-CDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs (Abs), and the excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of N-CDs; the inset shows the photo of N-CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm ultra-violet light (right), and (B) λex-dependent fluorescence emission behavior of N-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FL intensity of biomass N-CDs after storage for (A) 110 min, and (B) 2 months.

(A) The fluorescence (a) excitation and (b) emission spectra of biomass N-CDs, and (c) the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs; (B) Zeta potentials of N-CDs and AuNPs; (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs, and N-CDs-AuNPs; (D) FL emission spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs-AuNPs, N-CDs-Thiram and N-CDs-AuNPs-Thiram.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of AuNPs (0–0.08 mg/mL), and (B) the corresponding linear calibration plot; the inset shows the corresponding linear curve; (C) The impact of different buffer solutions on the fluorescence responses of N-CDs; the inset represents the color of the reaction system; (D) The impact of incubation time of thiram on the FL recovery of N-CDs.
(A) FL intensity change of N-CDs in the presence of thiram and different pesticides at 100 ng/mL. (B) The linear relationship between [(F 2 -F 1)/F 1] and concentration of thiram (10–200 ng/mL).
Illustration of an AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent “on–off-on” nanosensor for thiram detection.
Comparison of the proposed fluorescent sensor with some previously reported methods for thiram.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: UHPLC-ED: ultra-high performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrochemical detection;
IE-SERS: in-situ extraction surface-enhanced Raman scattering;
NFC/Au@AgNPs: nanofibrillar cellulose coated with gold-silver (core–shell) nanoparticles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of thiram from the spiked Hawthorn samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Gold nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped carbon dots based fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive detection of thiram in hawthorn

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of highly efficient and sensitive methods for trace detection of pesticides in foods is in great need. In this study, a signal “on–off-on” fluorescent nanosensor was proposed based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and biomass derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for accurate qualitation and quantitation of thiram in hawthorn. The water-soluble orange peel-derived N-CDs were obtained through a facile one-step microwave-assisted synthesis strategy. The fluorescence of biomass N-CDs could be effectively quenched by AuNPs through inner filter effect (IFE), and recovered when AuNPs were aggregated. In the presence of thiram, N-CDs specially bound with AuNPs through Au-S bonds, causing the aggregation of AuNPs with a visible color change from red to blue, accompanying with the release of N-CDs and a recovery of fluorescence. The restored fluorescence intensity was relevant with the added amount of thiram in a concentration-dependent manner for reliable quantitation. Under the optimized conditions, the newly-developed fluorescent nanosensor exhibited high selectivity for thiram out of other six interfering pesticides, as well as excellent sensitivity with an ultralow detection limit of 4.7 ng/mL and a wide detection range of 10–200 ng/mL. The practical application of the established nanosensor in the spiked hawthorn samples confirmed satisfactory recoveries of 102.22–107.57% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 5%. The results indicated the feasibility, practicality and wide application prospect of the proposed fluorescent nanosensor for thiram in foods and other commodities.

ce:section.ce:para: Dithiocarbamate (DTC) pesticides are widely and frequently used as the broad spectrum bactericides against more than 400 types of pathogens with an annual global consumption of more than 35,000 tons. However, these pesticides, especially their metabolite like ethylene thiourea and propylene thiourea, are reported with serious toxicity [1]. Thiram (tetramethylthiuram disulfide) is a representative DTC fungicide in crops, vegetables, seeds and herbal plants, as well as the vulcanizing agents in industry because of their high insecticidal efficiency. However, the widespread use of thiram has caused poisoning and unpredictable threats to human health as it can exert central nervous system diseases, endocrine disruptive and carcinogenic effects through skin contact, drinking, inhaling, and dietary exposure [2-4]. Therefore, the European Union has established the maximal residue limit (MRL) of 0.1–10 mg/kg for thiram in some foods [5]. To meet this strict requirement, it is of far-reaching significance and necessity for quick and accurate thiram detection in diverse matrices by developing a facile and reliable analytical method.

Currently, chromatographic [6], spectrophotometric [7], voltammetric [8], and SERS [9] methods are mainly included for thiram detection with fascinating sensitivity and high stability. But, these methods require expensive instruments, complex sample treatment, tedious operation, large cost and time consumption, and experienced operator, which are not adapted for rapid on-site testing. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, fluorescent nanosensors have arisen as the promising candidates for on-site pesticide detection benefiting from their simple operation, fast response, excellent sensitivity, low cost, and good reproducibility [10]. In establishing the fluorescent nanosensing platforms, some effective luminescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots [11], are commonly used. However, they suffer from complicated preparation processes, expensive chemicals, and additional toxicity due to the contained heavy metals, limiting their broad application.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based luminescent nanomaterial, have attracted tremendous interests in constructing fluorescent nanosensors for detecting different pesticides due to their easy synthesis, good water solubility, tunable fluorescence emission, favorable biocompatibility, and low toxicity [12]. Diverse approaches have been considered for the synthesis of high-performance CDs. Of them, microwave-assisted synthesis by using a simple and available microwave oven is rapid, facile, easy to operate, cost-effective, and environment friendly [13]. Under the homogenous heating effects of microwave irradiation, different CDs from various resources can be obtained through the contactless heat transfer to the reactants, realizing rapid reaction in a short time [14]. In addition, the uniform distribution of microwave energy in the precursors can achieve size-controlled, low-impurity, and high-yield of CDs [15]. Various kinds of precursors have been employed for preparing CDs, and of which, sustainable natural biomass as carbon source has attracted growing interests for the easy availability and increased environmental concerns. They, instead of chemical sources, are easy to obtain in the market for cheap and green synthesis of diverse kinds of CDs [16]. However, it is challenging to develop an effective strategy for preparing N-CDs from the biomass with enhanced photo-electronic properties and biocompatibility to improve the detection sensitivity. Then, diverse heteroatoms, especially non-metal atoms like nitrogen without associated toxicity, have been doped to synthesize nitrogen-co-incorporated N-CDs, in which the organic small molecules containing amino groups were used as the co-precursors. These N-CDs display promising fluorescent superiorities and wide application prospects for fluorescent sensing of trace pesticides [17].

N-CDs are usually used as fluorescent probes for the sensitive detection of targets through different fluorescence quenching mechanisms, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). IFE is a radiative energy transfer model that relies on absorption of the excitation and/or emission energy of the fluorophores by absorbers [18]. Compared with other mechanisms, IFE exhibits the distinctive merits of simplicity, good flexibility and no covalent linkage [19]. N-CDs have become the excellent candidate fluorophores due to their size-tunable optical properties, which allow facile tuning on the spectra overlap with absorbers in IFE sensing [20,21]. Although N-CDs display promising potential in developing homogenous probes for pesticide detection, there is no report on biomass N-CDs prepared from natural materials as the fluorescence probes for thiram detection in hawthorn samples. As an important edible and medicinal food, hawthorn with high nutritional and medicinal values have obtained global attention and wide applications [22]. Due to the long-term and over-dose use of thiram pesticide, hawthorn have aroused alarming concern on its quality and safety [23].

Thus, in this study, a facile and economical microwave-assisted strategy was employed for rapid synthesis of water-soluble biomass N-CDs by using orange peel as carbon nitrogen source and o-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source (Scheme 1 A). The obtained N-CDs were characterized for their structural and optical properties by transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM, Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), as well as UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Then, a signal “on–off-on” fluorescent nanosensor with the as-synthesized biomass N-CDs as the fluorescence probes and AuNPs as the quencher were fabricated for sensitive sensing of thiram (Scheme 1B). Without target thiram, the strong fluorescence (Signal on) of biomass N-CDs was quenched (Signal off) by AuNPs through IFE. Upon the addition of thiram, it would bind with AuNPs through Au-S bonds, causing the aggregation of AuNPs with a visual color change from red to blue, and the release of N-CDs with a fluorescence recovery (Signal on). This newly-developed fluorescent nanosensing platform displayed high selectivity for thiram out of 6 different pesticides, outstanding sensitivity, and verified reliability in real hawthorn samples. This study provided a new insight for thiram detection in the fields of food safety control and environmental pollution monitoring.

Hawthorn samples was bought from Tong Ren Tang pharmacy (Beijing, China). Fresh oranges were purchased from Song Guo Market (Chongqing, China). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were obtained from Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). A M1-L211A Midea microwave oven was purchased in Guangdong (China). Ethylene glycol, sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and o-phenylendiamine were got from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Atrazine, pretilchlor, dichlorvos, alachlor, carbofuran, metolachlor, and thiram standards were obtained from Anpel (Shanghai, China). The buffer solutions used in the experiments were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All chemicals used are of analytical grade without further treatment. Ultrapure water (UW) from Wahaha Co. Ltd. (Hangzhou, China) was used throughout the experiment.

TGL 16 M Centrifuge was purchased from Yancheng Kite Experimental Instruments Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). VOR TEX-Vortex Mixer purchased from Haimen Kirin Bell Laboratory Instruments Co., Ltd. (Haimen, China). A Tecnai G2F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI USA, Inc.) was used for the TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of N-CDs at 300 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on XRD-6000 X-ray powder diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The surface structure and element composition of the samples were studied by FTIR-8400S infrared spectrometer (SHIMADZU, Kyoto, Japan) ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by Thermo ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo VG, USA). The ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) spectrum was measured by a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) with a scanning range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by a FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Techcomp, Shanghai, China) with excitation and emission slits set at both 2.5 nm. The fluorescence lifetime was obtained by a JYHORIBA FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Jobin-Yvon, Inc., America) with excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The zeta potentials of N-CDs were measured on a ZS Malvern Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of biomass N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biomass N-CDs were synthesized via a microwave-assisted pyrolysis method by using a microwave oven with orange peel as the carbon precursor and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source, respectively. Briefly, the obtained orange peels from fresh oranges were cleaned with deionized water and dried sufficiently in the oven at 40 °C for 24 h, which were then ground into powder and passed through a 24-mesh sieve. 1 g orange peel powder was mixed with 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine in a beaker and diluted with 20 mL of ethylene glycol. Then, the beaker containing the mixed solution was placed horizontally in the center of a microwave tray. The knob of the microwave oven was adjusted to “high fire” for heating for 2 min. Afterwards, the beaker was turned off for cooling in a fume cupboard. A dark brown solution was obtained at room temperature, followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected for further purification against ultrapure water in a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) for 48 h at room temperature, followed by lyophilization for 48 h. The freeze-dried powders were stored in a dried cabinet at room temperature for further use.

The synthesized biomass N-CDs were comprehensively characterized via different techniques regarding the morphology and size distribution, surface structure and element composition. The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra was measured for their excitation and emission characteristics. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of thiram in standard solution
ce:section.ce:para: After the AuNPs and biomass N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor was established, it was used for fluorescent detection of thiram in standard solution according to the following procedure. The freeze-dried biomass N-CDs powders were diluted with ultrapure water to a final concentration of 0.3 mg/mL. First, 30 μL of 0.3 mg/mL of N-CDs solution was mixed with 240 μL ultrapure water, the fluorescence (FL) intensity was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and an emission wavelength of 440 nm as F 0. Then, 30 μL of 0.3 mg/mL of N-CDs solution was mixed with 240 μL of 0.1 mg/mL of AuNPs and 30 μL ultrapure water, followed by fluorescence detection. The FL intensity of N-CDs quenched by AuNPs was determined as F 1. Then, 30 μL of different concentrations of thiram was added into the N-CDs and AuNPs mixed solution for a 5-min incubation at room temperature for FL determination, and the FL intensity was regarded as F 2. The FL intensity differences [(F 0-F 1)/F 1] and [(F 2-F 1)/F 1] was calculated for optimizing the added amount of AuNPs for quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs, and for quantifying thiram.

Regarding the fabrication of a highly-selective fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of trace thiram, six pesticide molecules including atrazine, pretilchlor, dichlorvos, alachlor, carbofuran and metolachlor were tested fortheir ability to recover the quenched fluorescence of biomass N-CDs by AuNPs aiming for selectivity evaluation. Herein, 30 μL of biomass N-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was mixed with 240 μL of AuNPs (0.1 mg/mL) and 30 μL of solution containing each interfering pesticide at 100 ng/mL. After incubation of the mixed solution at room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum of biomass N-CDs affected by each pesticide was recorded via a spectrophotometer. The differences of FL emission intensity without (F 1) or with (F 2) diverse pesticides were compared.

To validate the practicability of the proposed AuNPs and biomass N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor in real world application, the hawthorn samples were taken into account for recovery tests. The sample extraction was done by grinding the hawthorn using a powder machine. Three hawthorn sample powders (about 1.00 g) were added with low (5 ng/mL), medium (10 ng/mL), and high (20 ng/mL) concentration levels of thiram standard solution, respectively, then 2 mL of methanol added until the different concentrations of thiram solution was fully absorbed by hawthorn powders. Then, the mixture was vortexed for 5 min, sonicated for 1 h, then re-vortexed for 2 min, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. Afterwards, the supernatant was collected and diluted with ultrapure water to pass through a 0.22-μm microporous filter membrane. Finally, the obtained filter solution was shaken well and added into the N-CDs and AuNPs mixed solution for a 5-min incubation and fluorescent detection according to the above-mentioned procedure for quantitation of thiram based on the FL intensity difference [(F 2-F 1)/F1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of biomass N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the best conditions to synthesize biomass N-CDs by microwave way and obtain the strongest FL intensity, some critical parameters were investigated. Firstly, the effect of different added raw materials on the FL intensity of N-CDs was investigated. As was shown in Fig. S1, the FL intensity of N-CDs reached the highest level of 9325 a.u. when o-phenylenediamine was added into the reaction system of the mixture of orange peel powder and ethylene glycol. While the FL intensity of the reaction system with the addition of ultrapure water was the lowest (i.e., 1506 a.u.). The inset of Fig. S1 showed the fluorescence spectra of the biomass CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength when synthesized with different raw materials added into the reaction system. Consequently, the o-phenylenediamine was selected as the nitrogen source of raw material to be added in the reaction system during the following studies.

The amount of the o-phenylenediamine was further investigated. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the fluorescence of N-CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm reached the highest intensity level of 15,978 a.u. with the addition of 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine into the reaction system. Hence, 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine was considered to be added into the mixture of orange peel powder and ethylene glycol for synthesis of N-CDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized biomass N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above-optimized conditions, water-soluble biomass N-CDs were facilely synthesized through a microwave-assisted method with orange peels and o-phenylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, which were then comprehensively characterized regarding their structural, morphological, and optical properties via different techniques.

To elucidate the structural feature of the synthesized N-CDs, they were characterized by TEM, HR-TEM, and XRD examination. The TEM images in Fig. 1 A showed that the obtained biomass N-CDs gave uniform dispersion in water with spherical shape. Well-arranged lattice fringes could be observed with the interplanar distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1A inset), which proved that the synthesized N-CDs had a similar graphite-like crystalline structure with those previously reported N-CDs obtained by chemical reagent synthesis [24]. They were corresponding to the (101) planes of graphitic carbon, indicating the graphitic nature of N-CDs. HR-TEM images in Fig. 1B further confirmed the clearer spherical shapes of the biomass N-CDs, which were distributed with narrow sizes ranging from 1.05 nm to 5.95 nm with an average diameter of approximately 2.45 nm (Fig. 1C). This finding was further verified by the XRD patterns of the hydrophilic N-CDs in Fig. 1D, which exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21°, and was similar to the graphite lattice spacing, suggesting an amorphous nature [25].

The chemical composition and functional groups of N-CDs can be investigated by the FT-IR and XPS spectra. FTIR spectrum is very vital for surface structure analysis of N-CDs. As was shown in Figure S1, ﻿the broad absorption band peak at 3392 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum was assigned to the stretching vibration of hydrophilic groups, such as –OH or N–H group, while, that at around 1652 cm−1 was for C = O group [25]. The peak located at 2930 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C–H group, and the vibrational absorptions of C-O (carboxy group) and C-O (alkoxy group) groups were located at 1375 cm−1, 1073 cm−1, and 1043 cm−1, respectively [26]. Meanwhile, the peak at 1614 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of aromatic C = C group [27]. The results demonstrated the successful synthesis of N-CDs, which were composed of aromatic skeletons with various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface. These functional groups on the surface of N-CDs attributed to their good hydrophilic property.

Then, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was introduced to examine the elemental composition and chemical bonds of the obtained N-CDs from the deconvoluted XPS spectra in Fig. 2 . It could be found in Fig. 2A that the synthesized biomass N-CDs were mainly composed of C (61.33%), N (4.15%), and O (34.52%) elements. Three typical binding energy peaks at 286.49 eV, 400.11 eV, and 522.15 eV in the XPS spectra corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. For the high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2B, four observed peaks corresponded to the O-C = O group at 288.5 eV [28], the C = O group at 287.2 eV, the C = C group at 284.7 eV, and the C-N/C-O group at 286.2 eV [29], respectively. Fig. 2C represented the high-resolution peaks of N1s with three main peaks. The binding energy peak at 399.2 eV was attributed to the pyrrole N, at 401.1 eV to the N–H group and at 400.1 eV to the pyridine N [29]. For the high-resolution O1s spectrum in Fig. 2D, three peaks at 532.7 eV, 533.6 eV, and 532.0 eV were found corresponding to the C = O, C-O, and O–H groups, respectively [30].

According to the above-obtained results from TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS characterization, it could be confirmed that the synthesized biomass N-CDs possessed graphite-like crystalline structures decorated with abundant hydrophilic functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, on the surface. The rich functional groups were conducive to the specific recognition and binding ability of N-CDs with thiram molecule, providing the basic conditions for the highly-selective fluorescence sensing of thiram.

The optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs were studied through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3 A. As was illustrated from the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (Abs), two obvious characteristic absorption peaks were observed. The first absorption peak at around 273 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of C = O bonds and the second peak at about 330 nm was resulted from the n-π* transition of C = C bonds. The fluorescence spectra have indicated the maximum excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelengths at 360 nm (blue line) and 445 nm (pink line), respectively. The inset image in Fig. 3A showed that the synthesized N-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultra-violet light. As was presented in Fig. 3B, the Em wavelength of the biomass N-CDs exhibited nearly no shift when the Ex wavelength was changed from 320 nm to 400 nm, indicating that the N-CDs featured a λex-dependent excitation behavior.

Furthermore, the short- and long-term FL stability of the N-CDs was examined through recording the FL intensity of them at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 4 A showed that relatively stable FL intensities about 16,000 a.u. were monitored after continuous tests of the N-CDs within 110 min. Fig. 4B further illustrated that even after storage for 2 months (60 days) at 4 °C in a bridge the N-CDs exhibited satisfactory long-term stability with high FL intensity at 16,000 a.u. To sum up, the synthesized biomass N-CDs with high FL intensity and good stability could benefit to the fabrication of the highly-sensitive signal “on-off-on” fluorescent nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of the fluorescent nanosensor for thiram detection
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNPs on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the quenching mechanism of AuNPs on the fluorescence of N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 A, The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs with a broad absorption peak at 400–520 nm partly overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs, which might contribute to the quenching effect of AuNPs on the fluorescence of N-CDs. The insets showed the comparison of the N-CDs solutions before and after the addition of AuNPs under visible (up) and UV light (down), respectively. In view of the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching, it could be presumed that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by AuNPs was not caused by the electrostatic interaction, which was confirmed from their surface charge through determining the zeta potentials.

As shown in Fig. 5B, a negative Zeta potential of −3.81 mV for the N-CDs was measured, which might be attributed to the sufficient carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface. While, the AuNPs demonstrated a negative Zeta potential of −35.1 mV, which was due to the negative citrate ions on their surface. Then, a remarkable electrostatic repulsion would happen between the negatively charged N-CDs and AuNPs. And the fluorescence quenching of AuNPs on N-CDs might be due to IFE or FRET between them.

Then, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs, and the N-CDs-AuNPs mixture were compared in Fig. 5C. It could be found that the values of fluorescence lifetime were about 7.10 ns for N-CDs and 7.17 ns for N-CDs-AuNPs, which were almost same. These results further indicated that the quenched FL intensity of N-CDs by AuNPs was mainly attributed to the AuNPs through IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery mechanism of thiram on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Upon addition of thiram, AuNPs spontaneously aggregate accompanying with a color change from wine red to blue, resulting in the release of N-CDs and a consequent recovery of the quenched FL intensity by AuNPs. This process realized the “on–off-on” pattern of N-CDs fluorescence for thiram determination. Here, the FL recovery mechanism of thiram on N-CDs was further investigated through comparing the FL intensities of N-CDs, N-CDs-AuNPs, N-CDs-Thiram and N-CDs-AuNPs-Thiram in their FL emission spectra.

As illustrated in Fig. 5D, the FL intensity of N-CDs and N-CDs-Thiram was almost same, indicating that the addition of thiram into the N-CDs solution would not affect the FL responses of N-CDs. While, the FL intensity of N-CDs-AuNPs was remarkably lower than that of N-CDs of N-CDs-Thiram, which further confirming the FL quenching effort of AuNPs on N-CDs. What’s more, the FL intensity of N-CDs-AuNPs-Thiram was greater than that of N-CDs-AuNPs, illustrating that the added Thiram could effectively recover the quenched FL intensity of N-CDs by AuNPs. These results indicated that the fluorescence recovery process might be due to a certain interaction between AuNPs and thiram, rather than the direct impact of thiram on N-CDs.

Thiram is a type of DTC pesticide containing abundant disulfide groups [31], which display strong interaction with noble metals like Au and Ag via covalent Au-S of Ag-S bonds. Hence, the disulfide bonds of the thiram molecules are broken when they encounter AuNPs, forming two di-carbamate fragments that maintain the chemical equilibrium of the system by electron delocalization [32]. Then, the thiram molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs via the formed Au-S bonds, leading to an aggregation of AuNPs with a color change from red (dispersed AuNPs) to blue (aggregated AuNPs). Consequently, abundant N-CDs are released, presenting recovered and enhanced FL responses. Therefore, it can be judged that the interaction between AuNPs and thiram through Au-S bonds gives rise to the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. As a result, the recovered FL intensity in a thiram concnentration-dependent manner was measured for quantitation of thiram in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing conditions for thiram
ce:section.ce:para: For achieving the best sensing performance of the AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescence nanosensors for thiram detection, some crucial parameters including the concentration of AuNPs, incubation time of thiram and the type of buffer solution were optimized.

First, the added amount of AuNPs to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs was investigated. Fig. 6 A showed that the highest FL intensity of pure N-CDs without AuNPs was about 12,000 a.u., which decreased gradually with increasing the added concentration of AuNPs. A significant negative relationship between [(F 0 -F 1)/F 0] and concentration of AuNPs could be observed in Fig. 6B with a linear equation of y = 1.078x − 1.649, R 2 = 0.9815. The FL intensity decreased to the largest extent when 0.08 mg/mL of AuNPs was added.

Buffer solution is widely used in the sensing process to maintain the solution pH and prevent the influence of the changed pH on the FL spectrum and intensity of N-CDs. Different types of buffer systems show various effects on the FL sensing process even under the same pH condition. Here, the impact of UW and six kinds of buffer solution, such as HEPES (20 mM 2-[4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl]ethanesulfonic acid, pH 7.2), TE (10 mM Tris-HCl-0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0), MES (0.5 M 2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid hydrate, pH 5.5), PBS (8 mM Na2HPO4-136 mM NaCl-2 mM KH2PO4-2.6 mM KCl, pH 7.2–7.4), TBE (9 mM Tris-H3BO3-0.2 mM EDTA, pH 8.0), and TAE (0.9 mM Tris-CH3COOH-0.02 mM EDTA, pH 8.3) on the sensing performance of N-CDs-AuNPs system was examined. As shown in Fig. 6C, the maximum FL intensity change ΔF = (F 0 -F 1) was obtained when MES buffer solution was used, followed by the UW. However, the color of the reaction system turned blue in the MES buffer system (inset of Fig. 6C), which would interfere the accurate detection. Thus, UW was chosen as the buffer solution throughout the experiment for dissolving N-CDs to obtain the optimum sensing performance of developed fluorescence nanosensor.

The addition of thiram into the N-CDs-AuNPs system caused the FL recovery of N-CDs, therefore, the incubation time of thiram was crucial for the sensing process. The changes in the FL intensity ratio [(F 2 -F 1)/F 1] without and with the addition of thiram into the N-CDs-AuNPs sensing system for incubation of 25 min were investigated at an interval of 5 min. Fig. 6D indicated that the [(F 2 -F 1)/F 1] value reached a plateau after a 5-min incubation with thiram, verifying that the interaction between N-CDs and thiram would be stable in a dynamic equilibrium. Therefore, 5 min was selected as the best incubation time for thiram in the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference capability of the AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor was investigated to examine its selectivity. Other six pesticides, such as atrazine, pretilchlor, dichlorvos, alachlor, carbofuran, and metolachlor as the interfering substances at the same concentration of 100 ng/mL with thiram were selected to evaluate their recovery capability on the fluorescence of N-CDs quenched by AuNPs. Fig. 7 A showed that, under the same experimental conditions, the quenched FL intensities of N-CDs at 440 nm were almost unchanged after the addition of the six interfering pesticide compounds. And their corresponding ΔF=(F 2 -F 1) values were all negative, which were far lower than the FL response after the addition of thiram, illustrating that these interfering pesticide molecules could not combine with N-CDs to recover the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. These findings all indicated that the newly-developed AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor displayed specific response and outstanding selectivity for thiram detection over other common pesticides.

Based on the IFE-triggered fluorescence quenching by AuNPs and the target (thiram)-induced FL recovery, the AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor was constructed under the optimized conditions for quantitation of thiram. Different concentrations of thiram (10, 30, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 200 ng/mL) were added into the N-CDs-AuNPs system for a 5-min incubation. And the FL intensities F 2 and F 1 of N-CDs at 440 nm without and with the addition of thiram were recorded to calculate the ΔF = [(F 2 -F 1)/F 1] value. It could be found that the ΔF change was proportional to the added concentration of thiram ranging from 10 to 200 ng/mL with a linear calibration curve of y = 0.0013x + 0.0006, R 2 = 0.9961 (Fig. 7B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 4.7 ng/mL (obtained by the equation LOD = (3σ/k), where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank sample and k is the slope of the calibration curve), which was much lower than the MRLs recommended by the regulations in European Union (0.1 μg/mL), Unite States (7 μg/mL), and Canada (0.1 μg/mL) [5,33]. Furthermore, the comparison of the proposed fluorescent nanosensor with other reported analytical methods in Table 1 displayed the comparable or even better performances of the newly-developed sensing technique, providing a preferred solution for trace detection of thiram in real complex hawthorn samples.

In order to further verify the applicability and reliability of the newly-developed fluorescent nanosensor for thiram detection, the real hawthorn samples were taken into analysis through a series of spiking tests by using the standard addition method. The real hawthorn sample were grounded into powder with the addition of three concentrations levels (5, 10 and 20 ng/mL) of thiram, followed by the above-mentioned procedure for recovery tests to quantify thiram. As presented in Table 2 , satisfactory recoveries from 102.22% to 107.57% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 5% were obtained, indicating the good accuracy and applicability of this AuNPs and biomass N-CDs based fluorescent “on–off-on” nanosensors in real-world analysis of thiram in hawthorn samples.

In summary, a facile and inexpensive one-step microwave-assisted treatment was introduced for the rapid synthesis of water-soluble orange peel-derived N-CDs for the first time. The biomass N-CDs displayed excellent blue fluorescence properties under UV light through comprehensive structural and optical characteristics, as well as good water solubility, and excellent short- and long-term photo-stability. Then, a signal “on–off-on” fluorescent nanosensor was fabricated with the biomass N-CDs as the FL probes and AuNPs as the quenchers based on the IFE mechanism. Furthermore, based on the binding reaction between AuNPs and thiram through Au-S bond and visual color changes, the biomass N-CDs were released and the recovered FL intensity was recorded for accurate qualitation and quantitation of thiram. Under the optimum conditions, the newly-developed AuNPs and N-CDs based fluorescent nanosensor exhibited high selectivity for thiram out of 6 different pesticides, as well as a good concentration correlation behavior with a LOD as low as 4.7 ng/mL. The practical application for the detection of thiram in real-world hawthorn samples displayed satisfactory recoveries of 102.22–107.57% with RSDs less than 5%, confirming the superior reliability of the proposed fluorescent nanosensor for complex samples.

Based on the IFE mechanism between N-CDs and AuNPs, the proposed fluorescent nanosensor could be considered as a cost-effective and simple method for pesticide detection in complex matrices. It should be noted that although fluorescence quenching is not limited by the distance between the fluorophore and the quencher, the current IFE-based sensing techniques still require improvements for their future broad applications. First, more excellent fluorophore/quencher pairs could be focused. Second, when the extinction coefficient of the quencher like AuNPs was not high, the sensitivity of the proposed method would be limited. At last, IFE-based methods were susceptible to interference in complex matrices, which should be further modified or avoided to improve their accuracy and reliability.

Lizhu Fu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yujiao Hou: Resources, Methodology. Haipeng Guo: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chang Liu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Ying Li: Methodology, Validation. Boyu Jia: Validation, Investigation. Lidong Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision. Weijun Kong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81973474) and Beijing Natural Science Foundation (7192130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107253.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
